**Single source of truth:** `lzg.home/src/lib/theme.ts`

See [Design System](./docs/design/DESIGN_INSPIRATION.md) for full documentation.

---

## Workspace Organization Rules

These rules apply across all repositories in the Lazy Golfing workspace.

### Single Source of Truth Principle

Every piece of data/configuration should have ONE authoritative source:

| What | Source of Truth | Don't Duplicate In |
|------|----------------|-------------------|
| Brand colors | `lzg.home/src/lib/theme.ts` | Individual CSS files |
| SEO metadata | `lzg.home/src/lib/metadata.ts` | Individual page files |
| API types | `lzg.backend/src/` DTOs | Frontend type files |
| CSV data | `public/alpha/*.csv` files | JSON directly |

### Static Asset Organization

All static assets MUST be organized in appropriate folders:

```
public/
├── favicons/     # All favicon files
├── images/       # Logos, OG images, photos
├── patterns/     # SVG patterns, textures
└── fonts/        # Custom fonts (if any)
```

**Never place files loose in public/ root** (except site.webmanifest, robots.txt).

### Social Sharing Standards

| Standard | Specification |
|----------|--------------|
| OG Image Size | 1200x630 pixels (1.91:1 ratio) |
| OG Title | All lowercase for brand ("lazy golfing") |
| OG Description | Tagline: "lazy golf for lazy golfers!" |
| Safe Zone | Center 800x400 area for cropping |

### Documentation Hierarchy

```
/CLAUDE.md                    # Quick reference, critical rules
/docs/                        # Detailed documentation
  ├── plans/                  # Roadmaps, feature lists
  ├── research/               # Market research, competitors
  ├── technical-debt/         # Code quality tracking
  └── architecture/           # System design
/lzg.home/CLAUDE.md          # Frontend-specific rules
/lzg.backend/CLAUDE.md       # Backend-specific rules
```

### Naming Conventions

| Type | Convention | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| React Components | PascalCase | `Header.tsx`, `CourseCard.tsx` |
| Utilities | camelCase | `utils.ts`, `formatDate.ts` |
| API Routes | kebab-case | `/v1/golf-rounds` |
| CSS Files | kebab-case | `button-styles.css` |
| Assets | kebab-case | `og-image.png`, `logo-dark.png` |

---

*For detailed project-specific guidance, see the CLAUDE.md files in each subdirectory.*

# CLAUDE.md - Lazy Golfing Platform

> AI assistant guidelines for the LazyGolfing monorepo

**Last Updated:** 2025-12-06

---

## Quick Reference

### Most-Used Commands

```bash
# Frontend (lzg.home/)
npm run dev                     # Development server (localhost:3000)
npm run build                   # Production build (static export)
npm run lint                    # ESLint checks
npm run build:data              # CSV → JSON (legacy /alpha site)
npm run check:data              # Verify JSON is current

# Backend (lzg.backend/) - Run on VM (192.168.1.40)
npm run start:dev               # Development server with hot-reload
npm run test                    # Unit tests
npx prisma generate             # REQUIRED after schema.prisma changes
npx prisma migrate dev --name <name>  # Create migration
npx prisma studio               # Database GUI (192.168.1.40:5555)

# Production
pm2 logs lg-api --lines 100     # View backend logs
pm2 restart lg-api              # Restart after deploy
```

### Key API Endpoints

| Endpoint | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `GET /auth/google` | Start OAuth flow |
| `GET /auth/session` | Check login status |
| `GET /v1/me` | Get user profile (auth required) |
| `PUT /v1/me` | Update profile (auth required) |
| `GET /v1/rounds` | List rounds (auth required) |
| `POST /v1/rounds` | Create round (auth required) |

### Test Commands

```bash
# Test API connectivity
curl https://api.lazygolfing.com/
curl https://api.lazygolfing.com/auth/session
```

---

## Repository Structure

```
Lazy Golfing/
├── CLAUDE.md                    # This file - root AI instructions
├── docs/                        # Centralized documentation
│   ├── plans/                   # Product roadmap, feature roadmap
│   ├── todo/                    # Task tracking
│   ├── technical-debt/          # Code quality issues
│   ├── design/                  # Design system documentation
│   └── architecture/            # System design docs
├── media/                       # Brand assets (not deployed)
│   ├── logos/                   # Official logos and variants
│   ├── kikman/                  # Kikman sub-brand assets
│   └── archive/                 # Historical planning screenshots
├── lzg.home/                    # Frontend (Next.js 15 + React 19)
│   ├── CLAUDE.md               # Frontend-specific AI instructions
│   ├── src/app/                # Next.js App Router pages
│   ├── src/components/         # React components + shadcn/ui
│   ├── src/lib/theme.ts        # Centralized color config
│   └── public/alpha/           # Legacy static site (preserved)
└── lzg.backend/                 # Backend (NestJS API)
    ├── CLAUDE.md               # Backend-specific AI instructions
    └── README.md               # Backend documentation
```

### Documentation Hierarchy

| File | Purpose | When to Read |
|------|---------|--------------|
| `/CLAUDE.md` | Quick reference, critical rules | Always |
| `/docs/` | Detailed documentation | Planning, research |
| `/lzg.home/CLAUDE.md` | Next.js/React patterns | Working on frontend |
| `/lzg.backend/CLAUDE.md` | API/database details | Working on backend |

---

## Development Setup

### Environment

- **Code editing:** Windows machine (this repo)
- **Backend runtime:** Ubuntu VM at `192.168.1.40`
- **Production API:** https://api.lazygolfing.com
- **Production Frontend:** https://lazygolfing.com

### First-Time Setup

```bash
# Frontend
cd lzg.home && npm ci && npm run build:data

# Backend (on VM)
cd lzg.backend && npm ci
cp .env.example .env  # Configure environment
npx prisma generate
npx prisma migrate dev
npm run start:dev
```

---

## Critical Rules

### Golden Rules (Never Break)

1. **CSV is source of truth** - Never edit JSON in `/data/` directly
2. **Always run `npx prisma generate`** after schema.prisma changes
3. **Protected routes need `@UseGuards(JwtAuthGuard)`**
4. **All inputs validated** via class-validator DTOs
5. **Never commit secrets** - Check `.env`, API keys, tokens

### Before Completing Tasks

- [ ] Backend: Tests pass (`npm run test`)
- [ ] Frontend: Data check passes (`npm run check:data`)
- [ ] No security issues introduced
- [ ] Code follows existing patterns

### CSV Data Workflow

```bash
# 1. Edit CSV file
vim golfers/golfers.csv

# 2. Regenerate JSON
npm run build:data

# 3. Verify
npm run check:data

# 4. Commit BOTH files
git add golfers/golfers.csv data/golfers.json
git commit -m "data(golfers): update..."
```

---

## Architecture Overview

### Frontend Data Flow

```
CSV files (source of truth)     JSON (build artifact)       Browser
golfers/golfers.csv      -->    data/golfers.json     -->   fetch + render
leagues/leagues.csv      -->    data/leagues.json
tours/tours.csv          -->    data/tours.json
```

### Backend Module Structure

```
src/
├── auth/           # Google OAuth + JWT (cookie: lg_jwt)
├── profile/        # GET/PUT /v1/me
├── rounds/         # CRUD /v1/rounds
├── courses/        # Course management
├── prisma/         # Database service (global module)
└── audit-log/      # Action logging
```

### Database Models

| Model | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| `User` | OAuth identity (Google), 1:1 with Player |
| `Player` | Golf profile (handicap, GHIN), has many Rounds |
| `Round` | Golf rounds with stats |
| `Course` | Course data with API integration |
| `RoundLink` | Playing partner linking |
| `Role/UserRole` | RBAC |
| `AuditLog` | Action tracking |

### Authentication Flow

1. Frontend → `GET /auth/google` → Google consent
2. Google → Backend callback with code
3. Backend creates/updates User + Player
4. Backend signs JWT, sets `lg_jwt` HttpOnly cookie
5. Backend redirects to frontend success page
6. Frontend calls `/auth/session` to confirm

---

## Environment Variables

### Backend (.env)

```ini
PORT=3000
NODE_ENV=development|production
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:pass@localhost:5432/lazygolfing
JWT_SECRET=<openssl rand -base64 32>
GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID=<from-google-console>
GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET=<from-google-console>
GOOGLE_CALLBACK_URL=http://localhost:3000/auth/google/redirect
```

### CORS Origins (src/main.ts)

```
https://lazygolfing.com
https://www.lazygolfing.com
https://lazygolfing-com.pages.dev
```

For local development, add your origin (e.g., `http://localhost:8080`).

---

## Troubleshooting Quick Reference

| Symptom | Cause | Solution |
|---------|-------|----------|
| Table not rendering | JSON not loaded | Check Network tab for 404 |
| Stale data | Browser cache | Hard refresh (Ctrl+Shift+R) |
| CSV changes not showing | Forgot build | `npm run build:data` |
| `Prisma Client not found` | Schema changed | `npx prisma generate` |
| 401 Unauthorized | Invalid JWT | Re-login |
| CORS error | Origin not allowed | Add to `main.ts` allowlist |

---

## Documentation Links

### Development Resources

- [Master Todo List](./docs/todo/MASTER_TODO.md)
- [Technical Debt Register](./docs/technical-debt/DEBT_REGISTER.md)
- [System Architecture](./docs/architecture/SYSTEM_OVERVIEW.md)

### Planning Resources

- [Product Roadmap](./docs/plans/PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md)

### Project-Specific Guides

- [Frontend CLAUDE.md](./lzg.home/CLAUDE.md) - Next.js 15, React 19, Miami Vice theme
- [Feature Roadmap](./docs/plans/FEATURE_ROADMAP.md) - Comprehensive feature list
- [Backend CLAUDE.md](./lzg.backend/CLAUDE.md) - API endpoints, Prisma, authentication

---

## AI Working Principles

### Approach

1. **Read first, act later** - Understand existing code before modifying
2. **Follow patterns** - Match the style of surrounding code
3. **Test before completing** - Run tests, verify manually
4. **Security first** - Never expose secrets, validate inputs

### When to Ask

- Requirements are ambiguous
- Security implications unclear
- Breaking changes needed
- Performance trade-offs exist

### When to Proceed

- Implementation matches existing patterns
- Non-breaking additions
- Bug fixes with clear root cause
- Refactoring that preserves behavior

---

## Brand Guidelines

### Voice
- Tagline: "Lazy golf for lazy golfers!" + "Cart Golf Only!"
- Friendly, inclusive, self-aware
- Celebrate small wins and funny losses

### Miami Vice Color Palette

| Color | Hex | Theme Usage |
|-------|-----|-------------|
| Hot Pink | #ff0080 | Dark mode primary |
| Cyan | #00f0ff | Dark mode secondary |
| Purple | #9b59ff | Dark mode accent |
| Coral | #ff6b6b | Light mode primary |
| Orange | #ff7033 | Light mode secondary |
| Yellow | #ffbf00 | Light mode accent |

**Single source of truth:** `lzg.home/src/lib/theme.ts`

See [Design System](./docs/design/DESIGN_INSPIRATION.md) for full documentation.

---

*For detailed project-specific guidance, see the CLAUDE.md files in each subdirectory.*

+All static assets MUST be organized in appropriate folders:

+| OG Title | All lowercase for brand ("lazy golfing") |
+| OG Description | Tagline: "lazy golf for lazy golfers!" |

content.text:
# Start Task - Intelligent Workflow Router

Analyzes your task and automatically routes to the optimal execution strategy per CLAUDE.md's Intelligent Prompt Routing system.

## Routing Logic

**If no task description provided:**
- Run `/check-todos` to list outstanding work
- Await user selection

**If task description provided, analyze and route:**

### 1. Planning Tasks → `/create-plan`
**Keywords:** "plan", "design", "architecture", "multi-phase", "roadmap"
**Complexity:** Multi-phase features, complex architecture
**Example:** "Plan the analytics dashboard feature"

→ Invoke: `/create-plan {{task_description}}`

### 2. Decision-Making → `/consider:*` frameworks
**Keywords:** "should we", "which approach", "evaluate", "compare", "decide"
**Examples:**
- "Should we use Redux or Zustand?" → `/consider:first-principles`
- "Which features to prioritize?" → `/consider:eisenhower-matrix`
- "Is this the right architecture?" → `/consider:second-order`

→ Analyze decision type and invoke appropriate framework

### 3. Debugging Tasks → `debug-like-expert` skill
**Keywords:** "debug", "intermittent", "mysterious", "sometimes fails", "race condition"
**Example:** "Debug the intermittent calendar sync issue"

→ Invoke: `Skill(debug-like-expert)` with task description

### 4. Moderate Complexity → `/create-prompt` + `/run`
**Criteria:**
- 30min - 2hr estimated work
- 3-10 files to modify
- Clear scope but needs fresh context
**Example:** "Refactor the widget loading system"

→ Invoke: `/create-prompt {{task_description}}` then offer to `/run`

### 5. Multi-Stage Features → `/create-meta-prompt`
**Keywords:** "research then implement", "explore options then build", multi-stage
**Example:** "Research authentication options then implement the best one"

→ Invoke: `/create-meta-prompt {{task_description}}`

### 6. Simple/Direct Tasks → Execute directly
**Criteria:**
- 1-2 files
- <30min work
- Clear, straightforward
**Example:** "Fix the typo in the README"

→ Execute directly in current context

## Implementation

**Step 1: Parse Arguments**
```javascript
const taskDescription = "{{task_description}}".trim();

if (!taskDescription) {
  // No task provided - check todos
  return "/check-todos";
}
```

**Step 2: Analyze Task**

Examine task description for:
- **Keywords** indicating task type
- **Complexity signals** (file count, time estimate, scope)
- **Execution pattern** (single-stage, multi-stage, research-heavy)

**Step 3: Route to Optimal Workflow**

Based on analysis:

```javascript
// Planning tasks
if (matches("plan|design|architecture|multi-phase|roadmap")) {
  invoke("/create-plan {{task_description}}");
}

// Decision-making tasks
else if (matches("should we|which|evaluate|compare|decide")) {
  determineFramework(); // first-principles, eisenhower-matrix, etc.
  invoke("/consider:[framework] {{task_description}}");
}

// Debugging tasks
else if (matches("debug|intermittent|mysterious|race condition")) {
  invoke("Skill(debug-like-expert)");
}

// Moderate complexity - delegate to fresh context
else if (estimatedComplexity === "moderate") {
  invoke("/create-prompt {{task_description}}");
  // Offer to run after creation
}

// Multi-stage features
else if (matches("research then|explore then|multi-stage")) {
  invoke("/create-meta-prompt {{task_description}}");
}

// Simple/direct - execute in current context
else {
  executeDirectly();
}
```

**Step 4: Follow Through**

After routing:
- If created prompt/plan: offer to execute with `/run` or `/run-plan`
- If direct execution: follow standard workflow (plan → approve → implement)
- If framework: present analysis and next steps

## Examples

```bash
# Automatically routes to /create-plan
/start-task Plan the user authentication system

# Automatically routes to /consider:first-principles
/start-task Should we use WebSockets or Server-Sent Events?

# Automatically routes to debug-like-expert
/start-task Debug the intermittent widget loading failure

# Automatically routes to /create-prompt
/start-task Refactor the calendar view components

# Automatically routes to /create-meta-prompt
/start-task Research GraphQL options then implement the best approach

# Direct execution
/start-task Fix typo in README.md

# Check todos (no argument)
/start-task
```

## Notes

- **Transparent routing:** User just describes task naturally
- **Zero configuration:** Analysis and routing happen automatically
- **Optimal execution:** Each task routed to best-fit workflow
- **CLAUDE.md compliant:** Follows Intelligent Prompt Routing specification
- **Composable:** Routes to other slash commands and skills as needed

# CLAUDE.md - Frontend (lzg.home) > AI assistant guidelines for the Next.js frontend application **Last Updated:** 2025-12-06 **Version:** 2.0 (Next.js migration) --- ## Quick Reference ### Tech Stack - **Framework:** Next.js 15 with React 19 - **Language:** TypeScript (strict mode) - **Styling:** Tailwind CSS 3.4 - **Components:** shadcn/ui (New York style) - **State:** Zustand (when needed) - **Theming:** next-themes (light/dark) ### Environment - **Code editing:** Windows machine - **Backend API (dev):** Ubuntu VM at `192.168.1.40:3000` - **Backend API (prod):** https://api.lazygolfing.com - **Production site:** https://lazygolfing.com (Cloudflare Pages) ### Essential Commands ```bash # Development npm install # Install dependencies npm run dev # Start dev server (localhost:3000) npm run build # Build for production (static export) npm run start # Serve production build locally npm run lint # ESLint checks # Legacy data pipeline (for /alpha site) npm run build:data # CSV → JSON conversion npm run check:data # Verify JSON is current ``` --- ## Project Structure ``` lzg.home/ ├── src/ │ ├── app/ # Next.js App Router │ │ ├── layout.tsx # Root layout (Header, Footer, Schema) │ │ ├── page.tsx # Home page (content only) │ │ ├── globals.css # Global styles + CSS variables │ │ ├── about/page.tsx # About page (uses main layout) │ │ ├── (auth)/ # Auth route group (minimal layout) │ │ │ ├── layout.tsx # Auth-specific layout (no full nav) │ │ │ └── login/page.tsx # Login page │ │ └── [feature]/ # Feature routes │ ├── components/ │ │ ├── layout/ # Layout components (SINGLE SOURCE) │ │ │ ├── Header.tsx # Site header with nav │ │ │ ├── Footer.tsx # Site footer │ │ │ └── index.ts # Barrel export │ │ ├── seo/ # SEO components │ │ │ ├── JsonLd.tsx # Schema.org structured data │ │ │ └── index.ts # Barrel export │ │ ├── ui/ # shadcn/ui components │ │ ├── theme-provider.tsx # next-themes wrapper │ │ └── theme-toggle.tsx # Dark/light toggle │ ├── lib/ │ │ ├── utils.ts # cn() utility for class merging │ │ ├── metadata.ts # Centralized SEO metadata config │ │ ├── theme.ts # Brand colors configuration │ │ └── auth.ts # Authentication utilities │ └── hooks/ # Custom React hooks ├── public/ │ ├── alpha/ # Legacy static site (v1) │ ├── favicons/ # All favicon files (organized) │ ├── images/ # Logo, OG image, photos │ ├── patterns/ # SVG patterns (grid, etc.) │ └── site.webmanifest # PWA manifest ├── next.config.ts # Next.js config (static export) ├── tailwind.config.ts # Tailwind + theme colors ├── tsconfig.json # TypeScript config ├── components.json # shadcn/ui config └── package.json # Dependencies & scripts ``` --- ## Layout Architecture (Critical Patterns) ### Single Source of Truth for Header/Footer **RULE:** Header and Footer live ONLY in `layout.tsx` - NEVER duplicate in pages. ```tsx // src/app/layout.tsx - THE pattern import { Header, Footer } from '@/components/layout'; export default function RootLayout({ children }) { return (
{/* Persists across navigation */}
{children}
{/* Persists across navigation */} ); } ``` **Why this matters:** - Header/Footer don't re-render on page navigation (SPA behavior) - Single place to update nav links, branding, footer content - Consistent experience across all pages ### Pages are Content-Only ```tsx // src/app/page.tsx - CORRECT export default function Home() { return ( <>
Hero content...
Features content...
); } // WRONG - Don't do this: export default function Home() { return (
{/* NO! This is in layout.tsx */}
Content
{/* NO! This is in layout.tsx */}
); } ``` ### Route Groups for Different Layouts Use parentheses folders `(group-name)` to create route groups with different layouts: ``` src/app/ ├── layout.tsx # Main layout (full header/footer) ├── page.tsx # / - uses main layout ├── about/page.tsx # /about - uses main layout ├── (auth)/ # Auth group - different layout │ ├── layout.tsx # Minimal layout (no full nav) │ └── login/page.tsx # /login - uses auth layout └── (dashboard)/ # Future: Dashboard group ├── layout.tsx # Dashboard layout (sidebar) └── rounds/page.tsx # /rounds - uses dashboard layout ``` **Key insight:** The `(auth)` folder name doesn't appear in URLs. `/login` not `/(auth)/login`. --- ## SEO & Metadata (Single Source of Truth) ### Centralized Configuration All SEO metadata is defined in `src/lib/metadata.ts`: ```typescript import { createMetadata } from '@/lib/metadata'; // In any page: export const metadata = createMetadata({ title: 'Rounds', description: 'Track and view your golf rounds', path: '/rounds', }); ``` ### What's Included (Automatic) | Feature | Implementation | Location | |---------|---------------|----------| | **OpenGraph** | og:title, og:description, og:image | `metadata.ts` | | **Twitter Cards** | summary_large_image | `metadata.ts` | | **Schema.org** | WebSite, Organization schemas | `JsonLd` component | | **Favicon** | All sizes, apple-touch-icon | `metadata.ts` | | **Robots** | index, follow, googlebot | `metadata.ts` | | **Canonical URLs** | Per-page via path | `createMetadata()` | ### Schema.org Structured Data ```tsx // In layout.tsx or pages: import { JsonLd, websiteSchema, organizationSchema } from '@/components/seo'; // For dynamic pages (e.g., course details): import { createGolfCourseSchema } from '@/components/seo'; ``` ### Updating SEO Across the Platform | To Change | Edit This File | |-----------|---------------| | Site name, tagline, description | `src/lib/metadata.ts` → `siteConfig` | | OG image | Replace `/public/images/og-image.png` (1200x630) | | Twitter handle | `src/lib/metadata.ts` → `siteConfig.twitterHandle` | | Schema.org data | `src/components/seo/JsonLd.tsx` | | Page-specific meta | Export `metadata` from the page file | --- ## Public Folder Organization (Critical Rules) **RULE:** All static assets MUST be organized in appropriate subfolders. ### Folder Structure ``` public/ ├── favicons/ # All favicon-related files │ ├── favicon.ico │ ├── favicon-16x16.png │ ├── favicon-32x32.png │ ├── apple-touch-icon.png │ ├── android-chrome-192x192.png │ └── android-chrome-512x512.png ├── images/ # Photos, logos, OG images │ ├── logo.png │ └── og-image.png # 1200x630 for social sharing ├── patterns/ # SVG patterns and textures │ └── grid.svg ├── alpha/ # Legacy site (don't modify) └── site.webmanifest # PWA manifest (references /favicons/) ``` ### Asset Path Rules | Asset Type | Folder | Example Path | |-----------|--------|--------------| | Favicons | `/favicons/` | `/favicons/favicon-32x32.png` | | Logos/Brand | `/images/` | `/images/logo.png` | | OG/Social Images | `/images/` | `/images/og-image.png` | | Photos/Screenshots | `/images/` | `/images/screenshot-1.png` | | SVG Patterns | `/patterns/` | `/patterns/grid.svg` | ### Social Sharing Image Specs | Platform | Optimal Size | Notes | |----------|-------------|-------| | OpenGraph (FB, LinkedIn, Discord) | 1200x630 | 1.91:1 ratio | | Twitter Cards | 1200x600 | 2:1 ratio (1200x630 works) | | Keep safe zone | 800x400 center | For cropping variations | ### Never Do This ```tsx // ❌ WRONG - Files loose in public/ src="/logo.png" src="/og-image.png" src="/grid.svg" // ✅ CORRECT - Files in organized folders src="/images/logo.png" src="/images/og-image.png" src="/patterns/grid.svg" ``` --- ## Miami Vice Theming System ### Centralized Color Configuration All colors are defined in `src/lib/theme.ts`: ```typescript // Full brand palette export const brandColors = { pink: { hex: '#ff0080', hsl: '330 100% 50%' }, cyan: { hex: '#00f0ff', hsl: '184 100% 50%' }, purple: { hex: '#9b59ff', hsl: '270 70% 60%' }, orange: { hex: '#ff7033', hsl: '25 100% 55%' }, yellow: { hex: '#ffbf00', hsl: '45 100% 55%' }, coral: { hex: '#ff6b6b', hsl: '10 100% 65%' }, }; ``` ### Theme-Adaptive Colors (globals.css) ```css :root { /* Light theme uses warmer colors */ --brand-primary: 25 100% 55%; /* Orange */ --brand-secondary: 45 100% 55%; /* Yellow */ --brand-accent: 10 100% 65%; /* Coral */ } .dark { /* Dark theme uses neon colors */ --brand-primary: 330 100% 55%; /* Hot Pink */ --brand-secondary: 184 100% 50%; /* Cyan */ --brand-accent: 270 70% 60%; /* Purple */ } ``` ### Using Theme Colors ```tsx // Theme-adaptive (changes with light/dark):
// Uses --brand-primary → --brand-secondary // Static brand colors (always same): // #ff0080 // #00f0ff
// Pink → Cyan
// Orange → Coral → Pink // Utility classes:
// Text glow effect
// Glass morphism
// Background glow effects ``` ### Theme Toggle ```tsx import { ThemeToggle } from '@/components/theme-toggle'; // In header: // Renders sun/moon icon button ``` --- ## Adding shadcn/ui Components ```bash # Add a component npx shadcn-ui@latest add button npx shadcn-ui@latest add card npx shadcn-ui@latest add dialog # Components go to src/components/ui/ ``` ### Available Components (pre-installed) - `Button` - Primary UI button - Additional components added as needed ### Usage Pattern ```tsx import { Button } from '@/components/ui/button'; ``` --- ## API Integration ### Backend Communication The new site will use the same API as the legacy site: ```typescript // src/lib/api.ts (to be created) const API_BASE = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL || 'https://api.lazygolfing.com'; export async function apiRequest(path: string, options?: RequestInit) { const response = await fetch(`${API_BASE}${path}`, { credentials: 'include', // For JWT cookie ...options, }); return response.json(); } // Usage: const session = await apiRequest('/auth/session'); const profile = await apiRequest('/v1/me'); ``` ### Environment Variables Create `.env.local` for development: ``` NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL=http://192.168.1.40:3000 ``` For production, the default falls back to `https://api.lazygolfing.com`. --- ## Routing ### App Router Structure ``` src/app/ ├── layout.tsx # Root layout (Header, Footer, Schema) ├── page.tsx # / (home) - content only ├── about/page.tsx # /about ├── (auth)/ # Auth route group (minimal header) │ ├── layout.tsx # Auth-specific layout │ └── login/page.tsx # /login ├── (main)/ # Future: Pages with full nav │ ├── rounds/ │ │ ├── page.tsx # /rounds (list) │ │ └── [id]/page.tsx # /rounds/123 (detail) │ ├── leagues/ │ │ └── page.tsx # /leagues │ ├── tournaments/ │ │ └── page.tsx # /tournaments │ └── profile/ │ └── page.tsx # /profile ``` ### Route Groups Explained | Group | Layout | Use Case | |-------|--------|----------| | `(auth)` | Minimal header, no nav | Login, signup, forgot password | | `(main)` | Full header/footer | Most public pages | | `(dashboard)` | Sidebar + header | Authenticated user pages | ### Link to Legacy Site ```tsx // Link to old pages during migration: Sign In (Legacy) ``` --- ## Cloudflare Pages Deployment ### Build Settings - **Build command:** `npm run build` - **Build output directory:** `out` - **Root directory:** `lzg.home` ### Static Export The `next.config.ts` is configured for static export: ```typescript const nextConfig = { output: 'export', // Static HTML export trailingSlash: true, // /about/ instead of /about images: { unoptimized: true }, // Required for static }; ``` ### Output Structure ``` out/ ├── index.html # New home page ├── rounds/index.html # New rounds page ├── alpha/ # Legacy site (copied from public/) │ ├── index.html │ └── ... └── _next/ # JS/CSS bundles ``` --- ## Development Workflow ### Starting Development ```bash cd lzg.home npm install npm run dev ``` ### Creating a New Page ```tsx // src/app/courses/page.tsx import { createMetadata } from '@/lib/metadata'; // 1. Add page-specific metadata export const metadata = createMetadata({ title: 'Courses', description: 'Browse golf courses and view ratings, slopes, and reviews.', path: '/courses', }); // 2. Page is content-only (Header/Footer come from layout.tsx) export default function CoursesPage() { return (

Courses

{/* Content here */}
); } ``` **Checklist for new pages:** - [ ] Export `metadata` using `createMetadata()` for SEO - [ ] Don't include Header/Footer (they're in layout.tsx) - [ ] Use the standard content wrapper pattern - [ ] Add to navigation in `components/layout/Header.tsx` if needed ### Creating a Component ```tsx // src/components/course-card.tsx interface CourseCardProps { name: string; location: string; par: number; } export function CourseCard({ name, location, par }: CourseCardProps) { return (

{name}

{location}

Par {par}

); } ``` --- ## Styling Guidelines ### Use Tailwind Classes ```tsx // Good - Tailwind utility classes
// Avoid - inline styles
``` ### CSS Variables for Theming ```tsx // Good - uses theme variables
// Avoid - hardcoded colors
``` ### Responsive Design ```tsx // Mobile-first approach
``` --- ## Legacy Site (Alpha) The previous static site is preserved at `/alpha/`: - **URL:** https://lazygolfing.com/alpha/ - **Location:** `public/alpha/` - **Purpose:** Fallback during migration ### Legacy Files ``` public/alpha/ ├── index.html, profile.html, rounds.html, ... ├── js/auth.js # Authentication client ├── js/profile.js, rounds.js # Page logic ├── css/style.css # Custom styles ├── data/ # Generated JSON ├── golfers/, leagues/, tours/ # CSV + scripts └── scripts/convert-csv.js # Data pipeline ``` ### Maintaining Legacy Data The CSV → JSON pipeline still works: ```bash npm run build:data # Converts CSVs in public/alpha/ npm run check:data # Verifies JSON is current ``` --- ## File Naming Conventions | Type | Convention | Example | |------|------------|---------| | Components | PascalCase | `CourseCard.tsx` | | Pages | kebab-case folders | `src/app/my-rounds/page.tsx` | | Utilities | camelCase | `utils.ts`, `apiClient.ts` | | Hooks | use prefix | `useAuth.ts`, `useRounds.ts` | | Types | PascalCase | `types.ts` → `export type Round` | --- ## Common Patterns ### Client Components ```tsx 'use client'; // Add at top for interactivity import { useState } from 'react'; export function Counter() { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); return ; } ``` ### Server Components (Default) ```tsx // No 'use client' - runs on server export default async function Page() { const data = await fetch('https://api.example.com/data'); return
{/* render data */}
; } ``` ### Loading States ```tsx // src/app/rounds/loading.tsx export default function Loading() { return
Loading...
; } ``` --- ## Debugging ### Check Theme ```javascript // Browser console document.documentElement.classList.contains('dark') // true if dark mode ``` ### Check API Base ```javascript // Environment variable in browser console.log(process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL); ``` ### Common Issues | Symptom | Cause | Solution | |---------|-------|----------| | Hydration mismatch | Theme flash | `suppressHydrationWarning` on html | | 404 on routes | Static export | Check `trailingSlash: true` | | Styles not loading | Tailwind purge | Check `content` in tailwind.config | | API CORS errors | Wrong origin | Add localhost to backend CORS | | Build fails | Type errors | Run `npm run lint` first | --- ## Related Documentation - **Root:** [/CLAUDE.md](../CLAUDE.md) - **Backend:** [/lzg.backend/CLAUDE.md](../lzg.backend/CLAUDE.md) - **Design System:** [/docs/design/DESIGN_INSPIRATION.md](../docs/design/DESIGN_INSPIRATION.md) - **Feature Roadmap:** [/docs/plans/FEATURE_ROADMAP.md](../docs/plans/FEATURE_ROADMAP.md) - **Architecture:** [/docs/architecture/SYSTEM_OVERVIEW.md](../docs/architecture/SYSTEM_OVERVIEW.md) --- *This file was updated during the Next.js v2.0 + Miami Vice migration (2025-12-06).* *Architecture patterns (Layout, SEO, Route Groups) added 2025-12-06.*

content.text:
# Start Task - Intelligent Workflow Router

Analyzes your task and automatically routes to the optimal execution strategy per CLAUDE.md's Intelligent Prompt Routing system.

## Routing Logic

**If no task description provided:**
- Run `/check-todos` to list outstanding work
- Await user selection

**If task description provided, analyze and route:**

### 1. Planning Tasks → `/create-plan`
**Keywords:** "plan", "design", "architecture", "multi-phase", "roadmap"
**Complexity:** Multi-phase features, complex architecture
**Example:** "Plan the analytics dashboard feature"

→ Invoke: `/create-plan {{task_description}}`

### 2. Decision-Making → `/consider:*` frameworks
**Keywords:** "should we", "which approach", "evaluate", "compare", "decide"
**Examples:**
- "Should we use Redux or Zustand?" → `/consider:first-principles`
- "Which features to prioritize?" → `/consider:eisenhower-matrix`
- "Is this the right architecture?" → `/consider:second-order`

→ Analyze decision type and invoke appropriate framework

### 3. Debugging Tasks → `debug-like-expert` skill
**Keywords:** "debug", "intermittent", "mysterious", "sometimes fails", "race condition"
**Example:** "Debug the intermittent calendar sync issue"

→ Invoke: `Skill(debug-like-expert)` with task description

### 4. Moderate Complexity → `/create-prompt` + `/run`
**Criteria:**
- 30min - 2hr estimated work
- 3-10 files to modify
- Clear scope but needs fresh context
**Example:** "Refactor the widget loading system"

→ Invoke: `/create-prompt {{task_description}}` then offer to `/run`

### 5. Multi-Stage Features → `/create-meta-prompt`
**Keywords:** "research then implement", "explore options then build", multi-stage
**Example:** "Research authentication options then implement the best one"

→ Invoke: `/create-meta-prompt {{task_description}}`

### 6. Simple/Direct Tasks → Execute directly
**Criteria:**
- 1-2 files
- <30min work
- Clear, straightforward
**Example:** "Fix the typo in the README"

→ Execute directly in current context

## Implementation

**Step 1: Parse Arguments**
```javascript
const taskDescription = "{{task_description}}".trim();

if (!taskDescription) {
  // No task provided - check todos
  return "/check-todos";
}
```

**Step 2: Analyze Task**

Examine task description for:
- **Keywords** indicating task type
- **Complexity signals** (file count, time estimate, scope)
- **Execution pattern** (single-stage, multi-stage, research-heavy)

**Step 3: Route to Optimal Workflow**

Based on analysis:

```javascript
// Planning tasks
if (matches("plan|design|architecture|multi-phase|roadmap")) {
  invoke("/create-plan {{task_description}}");
}

// Decision-making tasks
else if (matches("should we|which|evaluate|compare|decide")) {
  determineFramework(); // first-principles, eisenhower-matrix, etc.
  invoke("/consider:[framework] {{task_description}}");
}

// Debugging tasks
else if (matches("debug|intermittent|mysterious|race condition")) {
  invoke("Skill(debug-like-expert)");
}

// Moderate complexity - delegate to fresh context
else if (estimatedComplexity === "moderate") {
  invoke("/create-prompt {{task_description}}");
  // Offer to run after creation
}

// Multi-stage features
else if (matches("research then|explore then|multi-stage")) {
  invoke("/create-meta-prompt {{task_description}}");
}

// Simple/direct - execute in current context
else {
  executeDirectly();
}
```

**Step 4: Follow Through**

After routing:
- If created prompt/plan: offer to execute with `/run` or `/run-plan`
- If direct execution: follow standard workflow (plan → approve → implement)
- If framework: present analysis and next steps

## Examples

```bash
# Automatically routes to /create-plan
/start-task Plan the user authentication system

# Automatically routes to /consider:first-principles
/start-task Should we use WebSockets or Server-Sent Events?

# Automatically routes to debug-like-expert
/start-task Debug the intermittent widget loading failure

# Automatically routes to /create-prompt
/start-task Refactor the calendar view components

# Automatically routes to /create-meta-prompt
/start-task Research GraphQL options then implement the best approach

# Direct execution
/start-task Fix typo in README.md

# Check todos (no argument)
/start-task
```

## Notes

- **Transparent routing:** User just describes task naturally
- **Zero configuration:** Analysis and routing happen automatically
- **Optimal execution:** Each task routed to best-fit workflow
- **CLAUDE.md compliant:** Follows Intelligent Prompt Routing specification
- **Composable:** Routes to other slash commands and skills as needed

# CLAUDE.md - Lazy Golfing Platform

> AI assistant guidelines for the LazyGolfing monorepo

**Last Updated:** 2025-12-06

---

## Quick Reference

### Most-Used Commands

```bash
# Frontend (lzg.home/)
npm run dev                     # Development server (localhost:3000)
npm run build                   # Production build (static export)
npm run lint                    # ESLint checks
npm run build:data              # CSV → JSON (legacy /alpha site)
npm run check:data              # Verify JSON is current

# Backend (lzg.backend/) - Run on VM (192.168.1.40)
npm run start:dev               # Development server with hot-reload
npm run test                    # Unit tests
npx prisma generate             # REQUIRED after schema.prisma changes
npx prisma migrate dev --name <name>  # Create migration
npx prisma studio               # Database GUI (192.168.1.40:5555)

# Production
pm2 logs lg-api --lines 100     # View backend logs
pm2 restart lg-api              # Restart after deploy
```

### Key API Endpoints

| Endpoint | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `GET /auth/google` | Start OAuth flow |
| `GET /auth/session` | Check login status |
| `GET /v1/me` | Get user profile (auth required) |
| `PUT /v1/me` | Update profile (auth required) |
| `GET /v1/rounds` | List rounds (auth required) |
| `POST /v1/rounds` | Create round (auth required) |

### Test Commands

```bash
# Test API connectivity
curl https://api.lazygolfing.com/
curl https://api.lazygolfing.com/auth/session
```

---

## Repository Structure

```
Lazy Golfing/
├── CLAUDE.md                    # This file - root AI instructions
├── docs/                        # Centralized documentation
│   ├── plans/                   # Product roadmap, feature roadmap
│   ├── todo/                    # Task tracking
│   ├── technical-debt/          # Code quality issues
│   ├── design/                  # Design system documentation
│   └── architecture/            # System design docs
├── media/                       # Brand assets (not deployed)
│   ├── logos/                   # Official logos and variants
│   ├── kikman/                  # Kikman sub-brand assets
│   └── archive/                 # Historical planning screenshots
├── lzg.home/                    # Frontend (Next.js 15 + React 19)
│   ├── CLAUDE.md               # Frontend-specific AI instructions
│   ├── src/app/                # Next.js App Router pages
│   ├── src/components/         # React components + shadcn/ui
│   ├── src/lib/theme.ts        # Centralized color config
│   └── public/alpha/           # Legacy static site (preserved)
└── lzg.backend/                 # Backend (NestJS API)
    ├── CLAUDE.md               # Backend-specific AI instructions
    └── README.md               # Backend documentation
```

### Documentation Hierarchy

| File | Purpose | When to Read |
|------|---------|--------------|
| `/CLAUDE.md` | Quick reference, critical rules | Always |
| `/docs/` | Detailed documentation | Planning, research |
| `/lzg.home/CLAUDE.md` | Next.js/React patterns | Working on frontend |
| `/lzg.backend/CLAUDE.md` | API/database details | Working on backend |

---

## Development Setup

### Environment

- **Code editing:** Windows machine (this repo)
- **Backend runtime:** Ubuntu VM at `192.168.1.40`
- **Production API:** https://api.lazygolfing.com
- **Production Frontend:** https://lazygolfing.com

### First-Time Setup

```bash
# Frontend
cd lzg.home && npm ci && npm run build:data

# Backend (on VM)
cd lzg.backend && npm ci
cp .env.example .env  # Configure environment
npx prisma generate
npx prisma migrate dev
npm run start:dev
```

---

## Critical Rules

### Golden Rules (Never Break)

1. **CSV is source of truth** - Never edit JSON in `/data/` directly
2. **Always run `npx prisma generate`** after schema.prisma changes
3. **Protected routes need `@UseGuards(JwtAuthGuard)`**
4. **All inputs validated** via class-validator DTOs
5. **Never commit secrets** - Check `.env`, API keys, tokens

### Before Completing Tasks

- [ ] Backend: Tests pass (`npm run test`)
- [ ] Frontend: Data check passes (`npm run check:data`)
- [ ] No security issues introduced
- [ ] Code follows existing patterns

### CSV Data Workflow

```bash
# 1. Edit CSV file
vim golfers/golfers.csv

# 2. Regenerate JSON
npm run build:data

# 3. Verify
npm run check:data

# 4. Commit BOTH files
git add golfers/golfers.csv data/golfers.json
git commit -m "data(golfers): update..."
```

---

## Architecture Overview

### Frontend Data Flow

```
CSV files (source of truth)     JSON (build artifact)       Browser
golfers/golfers.csv      -->    data/golfers.json     -->   fetch + render
leagues/leagues.csv      -->    data/leagues.json
tours/tours.csv          -->    data/tours.json
```

### Backend Module Structure

```
src/
├── auth/           # Google OAuth + JWT (cookie: lg_jwt)
├── profile/        # GET/PUT /v1/me
├── rounds/         # CRUD /v1/rounds
├── courses/        # Course management
├── prisma/         # Database service (global module)
└── audit-log/      # Action logging
```

### Database Models

| Model | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| `User` | OAuth identity (Google), 1:1 with Player |
| `Player` | Golf profile (handicap, GHIN), has many Rounds |
| `Round` | Golf rounds with stats |
| `Course` | Course data with API integration |
| `RoundLink` | Playing partner linking |
| `Role/UserRole` | RBAC |
| `AuditLog` | Action tracking |

### Authentication Flow

1. Frontend → `GET /auth/google` → Google consent
2. Google → Backend callback with code
3. Backend creates/updates User + Player
4. Backend signs JWT, sets `lg_jwt` HttpOnly cookie
5. Backend redirects to frontend success page
6. Frontend calls `/auth/session` to confirm

---

## Environment Variables

### Backend (.env)

```ini
PORT=3000
NODE_ENV=development|production
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:pass@localhost:5432/lazygolfing
JWT_SECRET=<openssl rand -base64 32>
GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID=<from-google-console>
GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET=<from-google-console>
GOOGLE_CALLBACK_URL=http://localhost:3000/auth/google/redirect
```

### CORS Origins (src/main.ts)

```
https://lazygolfing.com
https://www.lazygolfing.com
https://lazygolfing-com.pages.dev
```

For local development, add your origin (e.g., `http://localhost:8080`).

---

## Troubleshooting Quick Reference

| Symptom | Cause | Solution |
|---------|-------|----------|
| Table not rendering | JSON not loaded | Check Network tab for 404 |
| Stale data | Browser cache | Hard refresh (Ctrl+Shift+R) |
| CSV changes not showing | Forgot build | `npm run build:data` |
| `Prisma Client not found` | Schema changed | `npx prisma generate` |
| 401 Unauthorized | Invalid JWT | Re-login |
| CORS error | Origin not allowed | Add to `main.ts` allowlist |

---

- [Backend CLAUDE.md](./lzg.backend/CLAUDE.md) - API endpoints, Prisma, authentication

## AI Working Principles

### Approach

1. **Read first, act later** - Understand existing code before modifying
2. **Follow patterns** - Match the style of surrounding code
3. **Test before completing** - Run tests, verify manually
4. **Security first** - Never expose secrets, validate inputs

### When to Ask

- Requirements are ambiguous
- Security implications unclear
- Breaking changes needed
- Performance trade-offs exist

### When to Proceed

- Implementation matches existing patterns
- Non-breaking additions
- Bug fixes with clear root cause
- Refactoring that preserves behavior

---

## Brand Guidelines

### Voice
- Tagline: "Lazy golf for lazy golfers!" + "Cart Golf Only!"
- Friendly, inclusive, self-aware
- Celebrate small wins and funny losses

### Miami Vice Color Palette

| Color | Hex | Theme Usage |
|-------|-----|-------------|
| Hot Pink | #ff0080 | Dark mode primary |
| Cyan | #00f0ff | Dark mode secondary |
| Purple | #9b59ff | Dark mode accent |
| Coral | #ff6b6b | Light mode primary |
| Orange | #ff7033 | Light mode secondary |
| Yellow | #ffbf00 | Light mode accent |

**Single source of truth:** `lzg.home/src/lib/theme.ts`

See [Design System](./docs/design/DESIGN_INSPIRATION.md) for full documentation.

---

## Workspace Organization Rules

These rules apply across all repositories in the Lazy Golfing workspace.

### Single Source of Truth Principle

Every piece of data/configuration should have ONE authoritative source:

| What | Source of Truth | Don't Duplicate In |
|------|----------------|-------------------|
| Brand colors | `lzg.home/src/lib/theme.ts` | Individual CSS files |
| SEO metadata | `lzg.home/src/lib/metadata.ts` | Individual page files |
| API types | `lzg.backend/src/` DTOs | Frontend type files |
| CSV data | `public/alpha/*.csv` files | JSON directly |

### Static Asset Organization

All static assets MUST be organized in appropriate folders:

```
public/
├── favicons/     # All favicon files
├── images/       # Logos, OG images, photos
├── patterns/     # SVG patterns, textures
└── fonts/        # Custom fonts (if any)
```

**Never place files loose in public/ root** (except site.webmanifest, robots.txt).

### Social Sharing Standards

| Standard | Specification |
|----------|--------------|
| OG Image Size | 1200x630 pixels (1.91:1 ratio) |
| OG Title | All lowercase for brand ("lazy golfing") |
| OG Description | Tagline: "lazy golf for lazy golfers!" |
| Safe Zone | Center 800x400 area for cropping |

### Documentation Hierarchy

```
/CLAUDE.md                    # Quick reference, critical rules
/docs/                        # Detailed documentation
  ├── plans/                  # Roadmaps, feature lists
  ├── research/               # Market research, competitors
  ├── technical-debt/         # Code quality tracking
  └── architecture/           # System design
/lzg.home/CLAUDE.md          # Frontend-specific rules
/lzg.backend/CLAUDE.md       # Backend-specific rules
```

### Naming Conventions

| Type | Convention | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| React Components | PascalCase | `Header.tsx`, `CourseCard.tsx` |
| Utilities | camelCase | `utils.ts`, `formatDate.ts` |
| API Routes | kebab-case | `/v1/golf-rounds` |
| CSS Files | kebab-case | `button-styles.css` |
| Assets | kebab-case | `og-image.png`, `logo-dark.png` |

---

*For detailed project-specific guidance, see the CLAUDE.md files in each subdirectory.*

content.text:
# Start Task - Intelligent Workflow Router

Analyzes your task and automatically routes to the optimal execution strategy per CLAUDE.md's Intelligent Prompt Routing system.

## Routing Logic

**If no task description provided:**
- Run `/check-todos` to list outstanding work
- Await user selection

**If task description provided, analyze and route:**

### 1. Planning Tasks → `/create-plan`
**Keywords:** "plan", "design", "architecture", "multi-phase", "roadmap"
**Complexity:** Multi-phase features, complex architecture
**Example:** "Plan the analytics dashboard feature"

→ Invoke: `/create-plan {{task_description}}`

### 2. Decision-Making → `/consider:*` frameworks
**Keywords:** "should we", "which approach", "evaluate", "compare", "decide"
**Examples:**
- "Should we use Redux or Zustand?" → `/consider:first-principles`
- "Which features to prioritize?" → `/consider:eisenhower-matrix`
- "Is this the right architecture?" → `/consider:second-order`

→ Analyze decision type and invoke appropriate framework

### 3. Debugging Tasks → `debug-like-expert` skill
**Keywords:** "debug", "intermittent", "mysterious", "sometimes fails", "race condition"
**Example:** "Debug the intermittent calendar sync issue"

→ Invoke: `Skill(debug-like-expert)` with task description

### 4. Moderate Complexity → `/create-prompt` + `/run`
**Criteria:**
- 30min - 2hr estimated work
- 3-10 files to modify
- Clear scope but needs fresh context
**Example:** "Refactor the widget loading system"

→ Invoke: `/create-prompt {{task_description}}` then offer to `/run`

### 5. Multi-Stage Features → `/create-meta-prompt`
**Keywords:** "research then implement", "explore options then build", multi-stage
**Example:** "Research authentication options then implement the best one"

→ Invoke: `/create-meta-prompt {{task_description}}`

### 6. Simple/Direct Tasks → Execute directly
**Criteria:**
- 1-2 files
- <30min work
- Clear, straightforward
**Example:** "Fix the typo in the README"

→ Execute directly in current context

## Implementation

**Step 1: Parse Arguments**
```javascript
const taskDescription = "{{task_description}}".trim();

if (!taskDescription) {
  // No task provided - check todos
  return "/check-todos";
}
```

**Step 2: Analyze Task**

Examine task description for:
- **Keywords** indicating task type
- **Complexity signals** (file count, time estimate, scope)
- **Execution pattern** (single-stage, multi-stage, research-heavy)

**Step 3: Route to Optimal Workflow**

Based on analysis:

```javascript
// Planning tasks
if (matches("plan|design|architecture|multi-phase|roadmap")) {
  invoke("/create-plan {{task_description}}");
}

// Decision-making tasks
else if (matches("should we|which|evaluate|compare|decide")) {
  determineFramework(); // first-principles, eisenhower-matrix, etc.
  invoke("/consider:[framework] {{task_description}}");
}

// Debugging tasks
else if (matches("debug|intermittent|mysterious|race condition")) {
  invoke("Skill(debug-like-expert)");
}

// Moderate complexity - delegate to fresh context
else if (estimatedComplexity === "moderate") {
  invoke("/create-prompt {{task_description}}");
  // Offer to run after creation
}

// Multi-stage features
else if (matches("research then|explore then|multi-stage")) {
  invoke("/create-meta-prompt {{task_description}}");
}

// Simple/direct - execute in current context
else {
  executeDirectly();
}
```

**Step 4: Follow Through**

After routing:
- If created prompt/plan: offer to execute with `/run` or `/run-plan`
- If direct execution: follow standard workflow (plan → approve → implement)
- If framework: present analysis and next steps

## Examples

```bash
# Automatically routes to /create-plan
/start-task Plan the user authentication system

# Automatically routes to /consider:first-principles
/start-task Should we use WebSockets or Server-Sent Events?

# Automatically routes to debug-like-expert
/start-task Debug the intermittent widget loading failure

# Automatically routes to /create-prompt
/start-task Refactor the calendar view components

# Automatically routes to /create-meta-prompt
/start-task Research GraphQL options then implement the best approach

# Direct execution
/start-task Fix typo in README.md

# Check todos (no argument)
/start-task
```

## Notes

- **Transparent routing:** User just describes task naturally
- **Zero configuration:** Analysis and routing happen automatically
- **Optimal execution:** Each task routed to best-fit workflow
- **CLAUDE.md compliant:** Follows Intelligent Prompt Routing specification
- **Composable:** Routes to other slash commands and skills as needed

1→# Neuman Brain Claude Skills
     2→
     3→Custom skills optimized for Neuman Brain development following CLAUDE.md workflow and design principles.
     4→
     5→**Last Updated:** November 19, 2025

     7→
     8→---
     9→
    10→## 🎨 Design & UX Skills
    11→
    12→### neuman-brain-design-system
    13→**Purpose:** Complete design system reference - brand colors, typography, spacing, component patterns
    14→
    15→**When to Use:**
    16→- Implementing UI components
    17→- Choosing colors/spacing values
    18→- Creating animations
    19→- Ensuring brand consistency
    20→- Checking accessibility (WCAG AA)

    24→- Typography: Inter font stack, responsive type scale
    25→- Spacing: 4px base unit system
    26→- Components: Widget, Modal, Button patterns
    27→- Animations: GPU-accelerated (transform/opacity only)

# Neuman Brain Claude Skills

Custom skills optimized for Neuman Brain development following CLAUDE.md workflow and design principles.

**Last Updated:** November 19, 2025
**Total Active Skills:** 10 (4 Neuman Brain-specific + 4 customized + 2 unchanged document skills)

---

## 🎨 Design & UX Skills

### neuman-brain-design-system
**Purpose:** Complete design system reference - brand colors, typography, spacing, component patterns

**When to Use:**
- Implementing UI components
- Choosing colors/spacing values
- Creating animations
- Ensuring brand consistency
- Checking accessibility (WCAG AA)

**Key Content:**
- Brand colors: Magenta (#E91E8C), Cyan (#00C9FF), Purple gradients
- Typography: Inter font stack, responsive type scale
- Spacing: 4px base unit system
- Components: Widget, Modal, Button patterns
- Animations: GPU-accelerated (transform/opacity only)
- Glassmorphism effects and shadows

**Invoke:** Automatically when UI/styling context appears

---

### neuman-brain-ux-designer
**Purpose:** Professional UI/UX design expertise and design thinking

**When to Use:**
- Designing new features
- Improving existing UX
- Solving user problems
- Creating interaction patterns
- Optimizing perceived performance
- Conducting design reviews

**Key Content:**
- Design thinking framework (Jobs-to-be-Done)
- User psychology (Hick's Law, Fitts's Law, Jakob's Law)
- Visual hierarchy and gestalt principles
- Interaction design patterns
- Accessibility beyond WCAG
- Performance-driven design
- Design critique framework

**Invoke:** Request explicitly: "Use neuman-brain-ux-designer skill to critique this design"

---

## 🛠️ Development Skills

### neuman-brain-code-reviewer
**Purpose:** Comprehensive code review enforcing CLAUDE.md workflow and production quality

**When to Use:**
- Before committing code
- Completing features
- Reviewing pull requests
- Checking workflow compliance

**Key Checks:**
- CLAUDE.md workflow compliance (task files, reviews, tests)
- Security (no XSS, type safety, input sanitization)
- Performance (bundle size, GPU animations)
- Accessibility (WCAG AA, keyboard nav, ARIA)
- Privacy-first (IndexedDB, local-first)
- Design system compliance
- Surgical simplicity (minimal code impact)

**Invoke:** Automatically before commits, or request: "Review this code for Neuman Brain compliance"

---

### neuman-brain-feature-developer
**Purpose:** Guide feature development with architecture patterns and best practices

**When to Use:**
- Adding new features
- Refactoring existing code
- Planning major changes
- Understanding architecture

**Key Content:**
- Zustand store patterns
- IndexedDB usage and quota management
- Widget system and lazy loading
- Feature integration checklist
- Common patterns (stores, widgets, IndexedDB)
- Performance guidelines

**Invoke:** Request: "Help me develop [feature] following Neuman Brain architecture"

---

## 📝 Documentation & Analysis Skills

### codebase-documenter
**Purpose:** Create comprehensive, beginner-friendly documentation

**Customized for Neuman Brain:**
- Neuman Brain docs structure (`docs/technical/`, `docs/product/`, etc.)
- Privacy-first documentation (IndexedDB, offline capabilities)
- Performance documentation (bundle size, lazy loading)
- TypeScript strict mode examples
- Zustand store patterns
- Design system references

**When to Use:**
- Writing README files
- Creating architecture docs
- Adding code comments
- Documenting APIs
- Making codebase accessible

**Invoke:** Request: "Document this feature for Neuman Brain"

---

### tech-debt-analyzer
**Purpose:** Identify technical debt and CLAUDE.md workflow violations

**Customized for Neuman Brain:**
- CLAUDE.md compliance checks (task files, reviews, dates)
- Workflow debt (missing task files, unarchived tasks)
- Code quality debt (root cause not fixed, surgical simplicity violated)
- Neuman Brain-specific checks (privacy-first, performance, design system)

**When to Use:**
- Analyzing codebase health
- Creating technical debt register
- Assessing code maintainability
- Checking CLAUDE.md compliance

**Invoke:** Request: "Analyze technical debt in [area]"

---

### test-specialist
**Purpose:** Write comprehensive tests using Vitest + React Testing Library

**Customized for Neuman Brain:**
- Vitest patterns (not Jest)
- React Testing Library best practices
- User-centric queries (avoid implementation details)
- Test coverage requirements (Rule #13)
- Zustand store testing
- IndexedDB service testing
- Accessibility testing (keyboard nav, ARIA)

**When to Use:**
- Writing unit tests
- Adding regression tests for bugs
- Improving test coverage
- Debugging test failures

**Invoke:** Request: "Write tests for this component using Vitest"

---

### frontend-enhancer
**Purpose:** Generic Next.js frontend enhancement patterns

**⚠️ OVERRIDE WARNING:**
Neuman Brain has its own design system. This skill provides generic templates - always adapt to Neuman Brain requirements using `neuman-brain-design-system` and `neuman-brain-ux-designer`.

**When to Use:**
- Generic UI patterns (adapt to Neuman Brain)
- Next.js best practices
- Responsive layouts

**Invoke:** Use with caution, prefer Neuman Brain-specific skills

---

## 📄 Document Skills

### document-skills (4 sub-skills)
**Purpose:** Official Anthropic document manipulation skills

**Sub-skills:**
- `docx` - Word document creation and editing
- `pdf` - PDF manipulation and forms
- `pptx` - PowerPoint presentation creation
- `xlsx` - Excel spreadsheet operations

**When to Use:**
- Creating/editing documents
- Generating reports
- Exporting data

**Invoke:** Automatically when document context appears

---

## 🔄 How Skills Work Together

**Example: Adding New Widget**

1. **neuman-brain-feature-developer** → Architecture guidance (Zustand, Widget system)
2. **neuman-brain-ux-designer** → Design interaction patterns
3. **neuman-brain-design-system** → Apply colors, spacing, components
4. **test-specialist** → Write Vitest tests
5. **neuman-brain-code-reviewer** → Review before commit
6. **codebase-documenter** → Update docs

---

## 📖 Skill Invocation

**Automatic:**
Skills activate automatically when relevant context appears (e.g., design system loads when implementing UI)

**Explicit:**
Request skills directly:
- "Use neuman-brain-ux-designer to critique this layout"
- "Use neuman-brain-code-reviewer to review this code"
- "Use neuman-brain-feature-developer to help me build [feature]"

---

## 🗂️ Removed Skills (November 19, 2025)

Removed 19 generic skills not relevant to Neuman Brain development:
- brand-analyzer, business-analytics-reporter, business-document-generator
- cicd-pipeline-generator, csv-data-visualizer, data-analyst
- docker-containerization, finance-manager, nutritional-specialist
- personal-assistant, pitch-deck, research-paper-writer
- resume-manager, script-writer, seo-optimizer
- social-media-generator, startup-validator, storyboard-manager
- travel-planner

**Reason:** Focus on skills directly supporting Neuman Brain development workflow.

---

## 📚 See Also

- `CLAUDE.md` - Complete workflow and development principles
- `docs/technical/development_principles.md` - Privacy/security/UX requirements
- `docs/product/feature_roadmap.md` - Product vision and roadmap
- `.claude/automation-guide.md` - Workflow automation tools

---

**Questions?**
- Skills not working? Check skill names match exactly (lowercase, hyphens)
- Need new skill? Follow `.claude/templates/skill-template.md` pattern
- Skill conflicts? Review skill descriptions for proper use cases

- Adding code comments
- Documenting APIs
- Making codebase accessible

**Invoke:** Request: "Document this feature for Neuman Brain"

---

### tech-debt-analyzer
**Purpose:** Identify technical debt and CLAUDE.md workflow violations

**Customized for Neuman Brain:**
- CLAUDE.md compliance checks (task files, reviews, dates)
- Workflow debt (missing task files, unarchived tasks)
- Code quality debt (root cause not fixed, surgical simplicity violated)
- Neuman Brain-specific checks (privacy-first, performance, design system)

**When to Use:**
- Analyzing codebase health
- Creating technical debt register
- Assessing code maintainability
- Checking CLAUDE.md compliance

**Invoke:** Request: "Analyze technical debt in [area]"

---

### test-specialist
**Purpose:** Write comprehensive tests using Vitest + React Testing Library

**Customized for Neuman Brain:**
- Vitest patterns (not Jest)
- React Testing Library best practices
- User-centric queries (avoid implementation details)
- Test coverage requirements (Rule #13)
- Zustand store testing
- IndexedDB service testing
- Accessibility testing (keyboard nav, ARIA)

**When to Use:**
- Writing unit tests
- Adding regression tests for bugs
- Improving test coverage
- Debugging test failures

**Invoke:** Request: "Write tests for this component using Vitest"

---

### frontend-enhancer
**Purpose:** Generic Next.js frontend enhancement patterns

**⚠️ OVERRIDE WARNING:**
Neuman Brain has its own design system. This skill provides generic templates - always adapt to Neuman Brain requirements using `neuman-brain-design-system` and `neuman-brain-ux-designer`.

**When to Use:**
- Generic UI patterns (adapt to Neuman Brain)
- Next.js best practices
- Responsive layouts

**Invoke:** Use with caution, prefer Neuman Brain-specific skills

---

**Purpose:** Official Anthropic document manipulation skills

**Sub-skills:**
- `docx` - Word document creation and editing
- `pdf` - PDF manipulation and forms
- `pptx` - PowerPoint presentation creation
- `xlsx` - Excel spreadsheet operations

**When to Use:**
- Creating/editing documents

- Exporting data

**Invoke:** Automatically when document context appears

name: codebase-documenter
description: This skill should be used when writing documentation for codebases, including README files, architecture documentation, code comments, and API documentation. Use this skill when users request help documenting their code, creating getting-started guides, explaining project structure, or making codebases more accessible to new developers. The skill provides templates, best practices, and structured approaches for creating clear, beginner-friendly documentation.
---

# Codebase Documenter

## Neuman Brain Project Context

**When documenting Neuman Brain code, follow these project-specific conventions:**

**IMPORTANT: Update documentation per CLAUDE.md Rule #11 - document changes immediately, never defer.**

### Documentation Structure
- **Technical docs:** `docs/technical/` - Architecture, implementation details, API specs
- **Product docs:** `docs/product/` - Vision, roadmap, feature maps
- **Business docs:** `docs/business/` - Business model, go-to-market, metrics
- **Strategy docs:** `docs/strategy/` - Competitive landscape, risk assessment
- **Security docs:** `docs/security_privacy/` - Privacy model, compliance

### Neuman Brain-Specific Requirements
- **Privacy-First:** All features must be local-first, document IndexedDB usage and offline capabilities
- **Performance:** Document bundle size impact, lazy loading strategies, GPU-accelerated animations
- **Accessibility:** Document WCAG AA compliance, keyboard nav, ARIA labels
- **TypeScript:** All code examples use TypeScript strict mode, no `any` types
- **State Management:** Document Zustand store patterns, persistence strategies
- **Design System:** Reference `.claude/skills/neuman-brain-design-system/` for colors, spacing, components

### Code Comment Style
```typescript
// ✅ GOOD - Explains why and context
// IndexedDB quota check: Prevents silent failures when storage is full.
// Without this check, writes fail with cryptic QuotaExceededError.
const quota = await indexedDBService.getQuota();
if (quota.percentUsed > 80) {
  showStorageWarning();
}

// ❌ BAD - Just repeats what code does
// Check if quota is over 80
const quota = await indexedDBService.getQuota();
```

### Architecture Documentation Pattern
When documenting Neuman Brain features, include:
- **Data Flow:** How data moves from UI → Store → IndexedDB
- **Performance Impact:** Bundle size, lazy loading, render optimization
- **Privacy Implications:** What data is stored, where, how it's encrypted
- **Integration Points:** How feature integrates with existing widgets/stores
- **Testing Strategy:** What to test (Vitest + React Testing Library)

### See Also
- `CLAUDE.md` - Workflow, development principles, core rules
- `.claude/skills/neuman-brain-design-system/` - UI patterns, colors, components
- `.claude/skills/neuman-brain-ux-designer/` - UX best practices, design thinking
- `docs/technical/development_principles.md` - Privacy/security/UX requirements

---

## Overview

This skill enables creating comprehensive, beginner-friendly documentation for codebases. It provides structured templates and best practices for writing READMEs, architecture guides, code comments, and API documentation that help new users quickly understand and contribute to projects.

## Core Principles for Beginner-Friendly Documentation

When documenting code for new users, follow these fundamental principles:

1. **Start with the "Why"** - Explain the purpose before diving into implementation details
2. **Use Progressive Disclosure** - Present information in layers from simple to complex
3. **Provide Context** - Explain not just what the code does, but why it exists
4. **Include Examples** - Show concrete usage examples for every concept
5. **Assume No Prior Knowledge** - Define terms and avoid jargon when possible
6. **Visual Aids** - Use diagrams, flowcharts, and file tree structures
7. **Quick Wins** - Help users get something running within 5 minutes

## Documentation Types and When to Use Them

### 1. README Documentation

**When to create:** For project root directories, major feature modules, or standalone components.

# Codebase Documenter

## Neuman Brain Project Context

**When documenting Neuman Brain code, follow these project-specific conventions:**

### Architecture Documentation Pattern

- **Performance Impact:** Bundle size, lazy loading, render optimization

---

## Overview

This skill enables creating comprehensive, beginner-friendly documentation for codebases. It provides structured templates and best practices for writing READMEs, architecture guides, code comments, and API documentation that help new users quickly understand and contribute to projects.

## Core Principles for Beginner-Friendly Documentation

When documenting code for new users, follow these fundamental principles:

1. **Start with the "Why"** - Explain the purpose before diving into implementation details
2. **Use Progressive Disclosure** - Present information in layers from simple to complex
3. **Provide Context** - Explain not just what the code does, but why it exists
4. **Include Examples** - Show concrete usage examples for every concept
5. **Assume No Prior Knowledge** - Define terms and avoid jargon when possible
6. **Visual Aids** - Use diagrams, flowcharts, and file tree structures
7. **Quick Wins** - Help users get something running within 5 minutes

## Documentation Types and When to Use Them

### 1. README Documentation

**When to create:** For project root directories, major feature modules, or standalone components.

**Structure to follow:**
```markdown
# Project Name

## What This Does
[1-2 sentence plain-English explanation]

## Quick Start
[Get users running the project in < 5 minutes]

## Project Structure
[Visual file tree with explanations]

## Key Concepts
[Core concepts users need to understand]

## Common Tasks
[Step-by-step guides for frequent operations]

## Troubleshooting
[Common issues and solutions]
```

**Best practices:**
- Lead with the project's value proposition
- Include setup instructions that actually work (test them!)
- Provide a visual overview of the project structure
- Link to deeper documentation for advanced topics
- Keep the root README focused on getting started

### 2. Architecture Documentation

**When to create:** For projects with multiple modules, complex data flows, or non-obvious design decisions.

**Structure to follow:**
```markdown
# Architecture Overview

## System Design
[High-level diagram and explanation]

## Directory Structure
[Detailed breakdown with purpose of each directory]

## Data Flow
[How data moves through the system]

## Key Design Decisions
[Why certain architectural choices were made]

## Module Dependencies
[How different parts interact]

## Extension Points
[Where and how to add new features]
```

**Best practices:**
- Use diagrams to show system components and relationships
- Explain the "why" behind architectural decisions
- Document both the happy path and error handling
- Identify boundaries between modules
- Include visual file tree structures with annotations

### 3. Code Comments

**When to create:** For complex logic, non-obvious algorithms, or code that requires context.

**Annotation patterns:**

**Function/Method Documentation:**
```javascript
/**
 * Calculates the prorated subscription cost for a partial billing period.
 *
 * Why this exists: Users can subscribe mid-month, so we need to charge
 * them only for the days remaining in the current billing cycle.
 *
 * @param {number} fullPrice - The normal monthly subscription price
 * @param {Date} startDate - When the user's subscription begins
 * @param {Date} periodEnd - End of the current billing period
 * @returns {number} The prorated amount to charge
 *
 * @example
 * // User subscribes on Jan 15, period ends Jan 31
 * calculateProratedCost(30, new Date('2024-01-15'), new Date('2024-01-31'))
 * // Returns: 16.13 (17 days out of 31 days)
 */
```

**Complex Logic Documentation:**
```python
# Why this check exists: The API returns null for deleted users,
# but empty string for users who never set a name. We need to
# distinguish between these cases for the audit log.
if user_name is None:
    # User was deleted - log this as a security event
    log_deletion_event(user_id)
elif user_name == "":
    # User never completed onboarding - safe to skip
    continue
```

**Best practices:**
- Explain "why" not "what" - the code shows what it does
- Document edge cases and business logic
- Add examples for complex functions
- Explain parameters that aren't self-explanatory
- Note any gotchas or counterintuitive behavior

### 4. API Documentation

**When to create:** For any HTTP endpoints, SDK methods, or public interfaces.

**Structure to follow:**

```markdown
## Endpoint Name

### What It Does
[Plain-English explanation of the endpoint's purpose]

### Endpoint
`POST /api/v1/resource`

### Authentication
[What auth is required and how to provide it]

### Request Format
[JSON schema or example request]

### Response Format
[JSON schema or example response]

### Example Usage
[Concrete example with curl/code]

### Common Errors
[Error codes and what they mean]

### Related Endpoints
[Links to related operations]
```

**Best practices:**
- Provide working curl examples
- Show both success and error responses
- Explain authentication clearly
- Document rate limits and constraints
- Include troubleshooting for common issues

## Documentation Workflow

### Step 1: Analyze the Codebase

Before writing documentation:

1. **Identify entry points** - Main files, index files, app initialization
2. **Map dependencies** - How modules relate to each other
3. **Find core concepts** - Key abstractions users need to understand
4. **Locate configuration** - Environment setup, config files
5. **Review existing docs** - Build on what's there, don't duplicate

### Step 2: Choose Documentation Type

Based on user request and codebase analysis:

- **New project or missing README** → Start with README documentation
- **Complex architecture or multiple modules** → Create architecture documentation
- **Confusing code sections** → Add inline code comments
- **HTTP/API endpoints** → Write API documentation
- **Multiple types needed** → Address in order: README → Architecture → API → Comments

### Step 3: Generate Documentation

Use the templates from `assets/templates/` as starting points:

- `assets/templates/README.template.md` - For project READMEs
- `assets/templates/ARCHITECTURE.template.md` - For architecture docs
- `assets/templates/API.template.md` - For API documentation

Customize templates based on the specific codebase:

1. **Fill in project-specific information** - Replace placeholders with actual content
2. **Add concrete examples** - Use real code from the project
3. **Include visual aids** - Create file trees, diagrams, flowcharts
4. **Test instructions** - Verify setup steps actually work
5. **Link related docs** - Connect documentation pieces together

### Step 4: Review for Clarity

Before finalizing documentation:

1. **Read as a beginner** - Does it make sense without project context?
2. **Check completeness** - Are there gaps in the explanation?
3. **Verify examples** - Do code examples actually work?
4. **Test instructions** - Can someone follow the setup steps?
5. **Improve structure** - Is information easy to find?

## Documentation Templates

This skill includes several templates in `assets/templates/` that provide starting structures:

### Available Templates

- **README.template.md** - Comprehensive README structure with sections for quick start, project structure, and common tasks
- **ARCHITECTURE.template.md** - Architecture documentation template with system design, data flow, and design decisions
- **API.template.md** - API endpoint documentation with request/response formats and examples
- **CODE_COMMENTS.template.md** - Examples and patterns for effective inline documentation

### Using Templates

1. **Read the appropriate template** from `assets/templates/`
2. **Customize for the specific project** - Replace placeholders with actual information
3. **Add project-specific sections** - Extend the template as needed
4. **Include real examples** - Use actual code from the codebase
5. **Remove irrelevant sections** - Delete parts that don't apply

## Best Practices Reference

For detailed documentation best practices, style guidelines, and advanced patterns, refer to:

- `references/documentation_guidelines.md` - Comprehensive style guide and best practices
- `references/visual_aids_guide.md` - How to create effective diagrams and file trees

Load these references when:
- Creating documentation for complex enterprise codebases
- Dealing with multiple stakeholder requirements
- Needing advanced documentation patterns
- Standardizing documentation across a large project

## Common Patterns

### Creating File Tree Structures

File trees help new users understand project organization:

```
project-root/
├── src/                    # Source code
│   ├── components/        # Reusable UI components
│   ├── pages/             # Page-level components (routing)
│   ├── services/          # Business logic and API calls
│   ├── utils/             # Helper functions
│   └── types/             # TypeScript type definitions
├── public/                # Static assets (images, fonts)
├── tests/                 # Test files mirroring src structure
└── package.json           # Dependencies and scripts
```

### Explaining Complex Data Flows

Use numbered steps with diagrams:

```
User Request Flow:
1. User submits form → 2. Validation → 3. API call → 4. Database → 5. Response

[1] components/UserForm.tsx
    ↓ validates input
[2] services/validation.ts
    ↓ sends to API
[3] services/api.ts
    ↓ queries database
[4] Database (PostgreSQL)
    ↓ returns data
[5] components/UserForm.tsx (updates UI)
```

### Documenting Design Decisions

Capture the "why" behind architectural choices:

```markdown
## Why We Use Redux

**Decision:** State management with Redux instead of Context API

**Context:** Our app has 50+ components that need access to user
authentication state, shopping cart, and UI preferences.

**Reasoning:**
- Context API causes unnecessary re-renders with this many components
- Redux DevTools helps debug complex state changes
- Team has existing Redux expertise

**Trade-offs:**
- More boilerplate code
- Steeper learning curve for new developers
- Worth it for: performance, debugging, team familiarity
```

## Output Guidelines

When generating documentation:

1. **Write for the target audience** - Adjust complexity based on whether documentation is for beginners, intermediate, or advanced users
2. **Use consistent formatting** - Follow markdown conventions, consistent heading hierarchy
3. **Provide working examples** - Test all code snippets and commands
4. **Link between documents** - Create a documentation navigation structure
5. **Keep it maintainable** - Documentation should be easy to update as code changes
6. **Add dates and versions** - Note when documentation was last updated

## Quick Reference

**Command to generate README:**
"Create a README file for this project that helps new developers get started"

**Command to document architecture:**
"Document the architecture of this codebase, explaining how the different modules interact"

**Command to add code comments:**
"Add explanatory comments to this file that help new developers understand the logic"

**Command to document API:**
"Create API documentation for all the endpoints in this file"
